content:
# Mark Chapter 10 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 10 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 10:2 — "divorce his wife" vs. "dismiss a wife" for ἀπολῦσαι γυναῖκα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if it-is-permitted for-a-husband to-dismiss a-wife" | Literal; ἀπολύω = send away/dismiss. |
| LOGOS | "Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife?" | Formal; standard English "divorce." |
| PNEUMA | "Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife?" | Same as LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "divorce" — while ἀπολύω literally means "release/dismiss," in this legal context it refers to the formal act of divorce. "Dismiss" is too casual for the legal gravity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 10:5 — "hardness of heart" vs. "your hearts are hard" for σκληροκαρδίαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For your hardness-of-heart" | Literal; compound noun. |
| LOGOS | "Because of your hardness of heart" | Formal; same construction. |
| PNEUMA | "because your hearts are hard" | Dynamic; resolves the compound noun. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Because of your hardness of heart" — the compound noun σκληροκαρδία carries OT prophetic weight and should be preserved as a unit.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 10:14 — "was indignant" vs. "was angry" for ἠγανάκτησεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "was-indignant" | ἀγανακτέω = to be indignant. |
| LOGOS | "he was indignant" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "he was angry" | Simplified; "angry" for ἀγανακτέω. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "was indignant" — ἀγανακτέω specifically conveys righteous displeasure, distinct from the more general ὀργή ("anger"). The nuance matters.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 10:16 — "took them in his arms" vs. "embraced them" vs. "picked up the children one by one"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having-embraced them, he-was-blessing them" | Literal; ἐναγκαλισάμενος. |
| LOGOS | "he took them in his arms and blessed them, laying his hands on them" | Formal; describes the action. |
| PNEUMA | "he picked up the children one by one, held them in his arms, and blessed them, placing his hands on each one" | Dynamic; adds "one by one" and "each one." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS — "took them in his arms" captures ἐναγκαλισάμενος accurately. PNEUMA's "one by one" is a reasonable inference but not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:22 — "His face fell" vs. "Having-become-gloomy" vs. "The man's face fell"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he, having-become-gloomy at the word, went-away grieving" | Literal; στυγνάσας = became gloomy/dark-faced. |
| LOGOS | "Disheartened by the saying, he went away sorrowful" | Formal; "disheartened" for στυγνάσας. |
| PNEUMA | "The man's face fell. He walked away devastated" | Dynamic; vivid; "devastated" intensifies. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "His face fell, and he went away sorrowful" — στυγνάσας literally means the face became dark/overcast, and "his face fell" captures this physical image. "Sorrowful" (λυπούμενος) is accurate; "devastated" (PNEUMA) over-intensifies.

**Status:** Resolved. Blended PNEUMA's "face fell" with LOGOS's "sorrowful."

---

## 10:25 — "go through the eye of a needle" vs. "thread a camel through" vs. "pass through"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for-a-camel to-pass through the eye of-a-needle" | Literal. |
| LOGOS | "for a camel to go through the eye of a needle" | Formal. |
| PNEUMA | "to thread a camel through the eye of a needle" | Dynamic; "thread" as active verb. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "for a camel to go through the eye of a needle" — PNEUMA's "thread" is clever but implies human effort, while the point is impossibility.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:33 — "Gentiles" vs. "Romans" for ἔθνεσιν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Gentiles" | Literal; ἔθνη = nations/Gentiles. |
| LOGOS | "the Gentiles" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "the Romans" | Historical specification; the Romans did the crucifying. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the Gentiles" — ἔθνεσιν means "the nations/Gentiles," and Mark uses this broader theological term deliberately. The Jewish-Gentile dynamic is theologically significant beyond the specific Roman identity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 10:38 — "drink the cup" / "be baptized with the baptism" — metaphor handling

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to-drink the cup which I drink, or the baptism [with] which I-am-baptized to-be-baptized?" | Literal; preserves both metaphors as-is. |
| LOGOS | "Are you able to drink the cup that I drink, or to be baptized with the baptism with which I am baptized?" | Formal; same metaphors. |
| PNEUMA | "Can you drink the cup I'm going to drink? Can you be plunged into the suffering I'm about to endure?" | Dynamic; "plunged into the suffering" interprets "baptism." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS — both metaphors ("cup" and "baptism") are retained without interpretation. PNEUMA's "plunged into the suffering" explains the metaphor but loses the specific term "baptism," which creates a powerful word-association with Christian baptism.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:42 — "lord it over" vs. "throw their weight around"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "those seeming to-rule the nations lord-it-over them" | Literal; κατακυριεύω. |
| LOGOS | "those who are considered rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them" | Formal; traditional rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "rulers throw their weight around, and powerful people push everyone else down" | Dynamic; vivid paraphrase. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "those who are considered rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and their great ones exercise authority over them" — the traditional rendering preserves the formal parallel structure and the ironic "considered" (οἱ δοκοῦντες).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:45 — "life" vs. "life" for ψυχήν — consistency check

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to-give his life [as] a-ransom for many" | "Life" for ψυχήν. |
| LOGOS | "to give his life as a ransom for many" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "to give his life as a ransom for many" | Same. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three agree: "life" for ψυχήν in this substitutionary atonement context. No disagreement.

**Status:** No disagreement.

---

## 10:48 — "telling him to be silent" vs. "told him to shut up"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "were-rebuking him, that he-might-be-silent" | Literal; ἐπετίμων + ἵνα σιωπήσῃ. |
| LOGOS | "telling him to be silent" | Formal. |
| PNEUMA | "People told him to shut up" | Colloquial. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "telling him to be silent" — "shut up" is too colloquial for the narrative register. The crowd's action is rebuking, not casual.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:51 — "Rabboni" vs. "Rabbi" vs. "Master" for Ῥαββουνί

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Rabboni" | Preserves the heightened Aramaic form from Sinaiticus. |
| LOGOS | "Rabbi" | Normalizes to the standard form. |
| PNEUMA | "Master" | Translates the title into English. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Rabboni" — this is the reading of Sinaiticus (ΡΑΒΒΟΥΝΙ), and the heightened form is significant. It appears only here and in John 20:16 (Mary Magdalene), both in moments of intense personal encounter.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET.

---

## 10:52 — "has made you well" vs. "has-saved you" vs. "has healed you" for σέσωκέν σε

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "your faith has-saved you" | Literal; σῴζω = save. |
| LOGOS | "your faith has made you well" | Healing context rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "Your faith has healed you" | Same as LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "your faith has made you well" — σῴζω in healing contexts means restoration to wholeness. "Made you well" preserves the double meaning (physical healing and spiritual salvation) better than either "saved" (too theological) or "healed" (too narrow).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal/theological vocabulary** (divorce, hardness of heart, indignant) | vv. 2, 5, 14 | Precise terms retained |
| **Metaphor preservation** (cup, baptism, eye of needle) | vv. 25, 38 | Metaphors preserved without interpretation |
| **Broader vs. specific terms** (Gentiles/Romans, Rabboni/Rabbi/Master) | vv. 33, 51 | Manuscript reading preserved; broader theological framing retained |
| **σῴζω rendering** | v. 52 | Context-sensitive; "made well" in healing contexts |
| **PNEUMA's colloquialisms** ("shut up," "devastated," "thread") | vv. 22, 25, 48 | Generally not adopted; moderated register preferred |
| **Emotional vocabulary** (face fell, sorrowful, indignant) | vv. 14, 22 | Precise Greek terms preserved |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 10 covers some of the most pastorally significant material in the Gospel — marriage, children, wealth, discipleship, and servanthood. The most significant disagreement is the rendering of Ῥαββουνί in v. 51, where SOPHIA sided with EMET's preservation of the manuscript form over LOGOS's normalization and PNEUMA's translation. This is consistent with the methodology of translating what the manuscript says. The chapter also shows strong three-way agreement on crucial theological formulations (v. 45, ransom for many), while diverging predictably on register and style.
